author: Ameen Hassan
summary: Session focused on cleaning up the AgentOS/ameen-context-mcp data layer. 2,233 garbage sessions were deleted from storage. The pending queue was also cleaned, keeping 19 valid entries and removing 2,631 stale entries. Synthesis was re-run via synthesize_memory.py to rebuild clean project_memory for the agentos project from the remaining legitimate sessions. The rebuilt memory state confirmed 22 known solutions, 23 decisions, and 33 open threads — consistent with the expected baseline. A fingerprint guard in session_end.py was tested with three cases: real user session (not blocked), structuring meta-session (blocked), and synthesis meta-session (blocked) — all three tests passed. A syntax check on session_end.py passed. Changes to hooks and data were committed to git.
problems: Garbage session entries had accumulated in storage (2,233 entries deleted)
Pending queue was polluted with stale entries (2,631 removed)
Project memory needed to be rebuilt from clean legitimate sessions after purge
approaches: Ran Python script to delete garbage sessions and clean the pending queue
Re-ran synthesize_memory.py --project agentos to rebuild project_memory from valid sessions
Tested fingerprint guard logic with three simulated inputs to verify meta-session blocking
Ran py_compile syntax check on session_end.py before committing
results: Pending queue cleaned: 19 valid entries kept, 2,631 stale entries removed
Rebuilt agentos project_memory: 22 known solutions, 23 decisions, 33 open threads
Fingerprint guard tests all passed: real sessions allowed, meta-sessions blocked

decisions.decision: Purge garbage sessions and stale queue entries before re-synthesizing memory
Fingerprint guard in session_end.py is the mechanism to block recursive meta-session processing

open_questions: Whether the 19 valid pending queue entries will be processed correctly in the next synthesis pass
Whether the git commit completed successfully (transcript was cut off during git add)
